| Port | HTTPS: '44300' (typical) | HTTP: '8000' for instance 00, '8001' for instance 01 |
 
Step 2.1 - Install and Configure ABAP FS
1. In VS Code Extensions ('Ctrl+Shift+X'), search ABAP remote filesystem by Marcello Urbani → Install.
2. Press 'Ctrl+Shift+P' → type ABAP FS: Connection Manager → open it.
3. Add a new connection and fill in the fields:
   - Name: anything you like (e.g. DEV or SANDBOX)
   - Server URL:'https://<hostname>:44300'
   - ⚠️ Do NOT add '/sap/bc/adt' to the URL - the extension adds this automatically. If you include it, the path doubles and the connection fails.
   - Client, Username, Language: 'EN'
4. Save.
5. Press 'Ctrl+Shift+P' → ABAP FS: Connect to an SAP system→ select your connection → enter password.
 

 

> ⚠️ SSL Certificate Error (very common on internal systems)
> If you see IP does not match certificate's altnames - this is because your SAP system's SSL certificate was issued with a hostname, not the IP. The fix: Get the hostname (e.g. 'https://sapdev.company.com:44300') and use that instead of the IP. 
> ⚠️ADT Service Not Found
> If you see Resource /sap/bc/adt does not exist - the ADT service isn't activated. Run tcode SICF → navigate to default_host → 'sap/bc/adt' → right-click → Activate Service.
 
Step 2.2 - Navigating ABAP Objects
ABAP FS gives you two ways to find objects. Object Search lets you find anything by name instantly. If you prefer a tree view like Eclipse ADT, enable SAP GUI Integration in your connection settings - this gives you a full package tree (Dictionary, Source Code Library, Transactions) and even embeds SAP GUI for HTML directly inside VS Code, so you can run transactions without leaving the editor.
1. Click OBJECT SEARCH in the left sidebar
2. Type the object or package name (e.g. 'ZCL_MY_CLASS' or 'Z_MY_PACKAGE')
3. Double-click any result to open it as a text file in VS Code
4. To edit: right-click  → edit → save (changes push back to SAP automatically)
Once an object is open in VS Code, open the integrated terminal (Ctrl+) and run claude. Some prompts that immediately pay off:

 

Part 3 -The Game Changer: Enabling the Native MCP Server

 

This is the step that changes everything - and the reason this post exists.
Why ABAP FS Alone Isn't Enough?
With only ABAP FS connected, Claude can see only the one file you currently have open. Ask it to create a new class and it might invent table names, function modules, or BAdI definitions that don't exist on your system. It's helpful, but it's guessing.
          ABAP FS Only                                         ABAP FS + MCP
Claude sees what we search Manually Claude autonomously searches any object on the system
Hallucinates objects it hasn't seen Grounded in live SAP system data
You copy-paste code manually Claude reads, writes, activates autonomously
No transport awareness Creates & assigns transports via ADT

 

The MCP (Model Context Protocol) server gives Claude Code a live connection to your SAP system's ADT API - the same backend Eclipse uses. Claude stops guessing and starts knowing.
Step 3.1 - Enable the MCP Server (One Toggle)
This is where the zero-config part comes in. You don't need to install anything extra, clone a Node.js repo, or edit JSON files manually.
Press 'Ctrl+Shift+P' → search ABAP FS MCP → select it → enable Autostart.
That's it. The ABAP FS extension spins up a local MCP server on your machine (default port 4847). You'll see a VS Code notification confirming it's running:
> ABAP MCP Server running on port 4847. External AI clients can connect to http://localhost:4847/mcp

Step 3.2 - Register the MCP Server with Claude Code
Now tell Claude Code where to find the ABAP tools. Run this in the VS Code integrated terminal:
This registers the local MCP server with Claude Code. The configuration is saved to your '.claude.json' file and persists across sessions - you only need to do this once.
Step 3.3 - Verify the Connection

 

open the terminal and run this command once the MCP server is added 
You should see:
Claude Code will confirm when the server is registered:
> abap-remote-fs has been added with HTTP transport at http://localhost:4847/mcp"*
The Complete Architecture
Claude Code can now autonomously:
- Search and read any ABAP object on the live SAP system
- write → syntax check → activate → unlock objects
- Create transport requests and assign objects
- Run ATC checks and read short dumps - all from a plain-English prompt
> Golden - In Auto mode, Claude will write, syntax check, and activate without pausing - that's the power of the agentic workflow. If you want to review before anything is activated, switch to Plan mode  shift+tab to cycle modes)  Claude will show you the full plan and wait for your approval. For production-adjacent systems, always run in Plan or Step mode.
Part 4 - Live Demo: One Prompt, One Real ABAP Program on SAP
Disclaimer: This is my first time setting up all this and explore, so i just started with a very simple scenario. Going forward will create RAP objects and other complex artifacts and see the efficiency.
Let me show you what all of this enables. Here's the exact prompt I used:
> Create a new ABAP executable program ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01 in my ZVS_DEMO package. Fetch data from MARA for a user-specified Material Number (select-option) and display Material Number, Type, and Created By in an ALV grid. Write in ABAP 7.5 syntax.
One prompt. No templates. No copy-paste. Here's what Claude did.
Act 1 - Claude Plans Before It Codes
Claude didn't immediately start writing code. It:
1. Confirmed the target package ('ZVS_DEMO') exists on the system
2. Detected that the program creation triggered a **transport request dialog** — and recognised it was the likely source of a cancellation
3. Called the SAP ADT API via Bash to **list existing open transports**
4. Selected the correct transport and retried the creation
There was a little bit delay on my end to confirm the transport. and it gave me this message.
> Package confirmed. The cancellation is likely a transport request dialog. Let me check for existing transports, then retry.
This is the part that impresses ABAP developers most. Claude handled a real SAP workflow quirk .
Act 2 - The Program Appears on SAP
Claude then created the program on the live system. You can watch its autonomous todo list mid-execution:
- Create ABAP program ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01 in ZVS_DEMO package via MCP
- Get workspace URI for the new program
- Write ABAP source code into the program
- Activate the program and check diagnostics
'ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01' appeared in the VS Code Explorer sidebar - a real, registered ABAP program on the SAP system, not a local file.
You can see initially there was no program created in my package 
After few seconds, the program is created 

 

Act 3 - Full ABAP 7.5 Source Code Written
With the object created, Claude fetched the workspace URI and wrote the complete source code directly into the SAP program. What it generated:
No placeholder code. Real, working ABAP in the correct syntax release, referencing actual MARA field types from the live system.
Act 4 - Running the Program
After activation, there are two ways to run it:
Option A - Classic SAP GUI:Log in to SAP GUI →run the program → Execute
Option B - Embedded SAP Web GUI(what I used): VS Code can open the SAP Web GUI directly in a browser tab, embedded inside the IDE. No separate login or window switching required.
Enter selection criteria:
The selection screen appeared with the Matnr  field exactly as specified. I entered a range of 11to 21. The value help (F4) shows the available materials in the system
Material Number, Material Type, and Created By - exactly what was asked for. Live data from MARA. Report running.
 
Summary:
|Claude understands SAP context| It identified the transport dialog, queried existing transports via ADT, and handled a real SAP workflow quirk autonomously 
| It created a real SAP object| 'ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01' exists on the live system, created via ADT REST API through MCP - not a file on disk 
| Production-quality ABAP 7.5 | TYPE declarations referencing actual MARA field types, correct naming conventions, modern SQL syntax 
| Minutes, not hours| The developer's job shifts to reviewing - not writing skeletons, hunting transport dialogs, or looking up field types 
| Fully iterative| Once created, you can ask Claude to add authority checks, error handling, ALV field catalogues, or additional filters - each iteration in seconds 
| You stay in control | Claude's todo list is transparent - you see every step it plans. You review the code. You decide what activates 
 
Where to Go from Here?
Here are five things to try once you have the setup working:
1. Understand inherited code - Explain the purpose and flow of report Z_*_REPORT. Include tables accessed, authority checks, deprecated patterns like SELECT .
2. Generate a CDS view - Create a CDS view hierarchy for Sales Order analysis: interface view joining VBAK+VBAP, projection view with Fiori Elements. UI annotations for a List Report.
3. Build a Travel Request POC -  RAP Business Object with CRUD, approve/reject action, draft handling, OData V4 binding, Fiori Elements UI.
4. Write ABAP Unit tests - Generate a unit test class 
5. Code review and modernization - Review this class for Clean ABAP violations, missing authority checks, SELECT * usage, and SELECT inside LOOP. Propose S/4-equivalent replacements.
 
The bottom line: The integration that used to require manual Node.js setup, JSON editing, and external package management now takes a single Ctrl+Shift+P toggle and one terminal command.
 
The ABAP Remote Filesystem team has done the hard work. Claude Code does the coding. You do the review.
Give it a try - and let me know in the comments what use case you tackle first.
 
A personal note  : This is my first time diving deeper into vibe coding and MCP concepts. I come from a classic ABAP background, and everything covered here -  from the VS Code setup to the MCP server to the live demo - I explored and built from scratch over the past few weeks. The scenario is intentionally simple. The goal was to get the fundamentals right, document them honestly, and share what works. There is a lot more ground to cover - RAP generation at scale, Clean Core extensibility patterns, ATC pre-checks via Claude Code, and deeper agentic workflows. I'll be going further in the posts ahead. If you're also just getting started with this, I hope this gives you a solid, working foundation to build on. And if you're already further ahead - I'd love to hear what you've learnt in the comments.
 
Note: All screenshots in this post are taken from a live S/4HANA development system. Code generated by Claude Code should always be reviewed and tested in a sandbox environment before activation in any productive system.

entry.title: Building a RAP Application with External SAP HANA Cloud using CDS External Entities – Part 1
entry.content:
Why External Entities?

CDS external entities provide a modern way to handle secondary database connections in ABAP Cloud. They allow applications to retrieve and modify data from other databases using SQL. Sounds interesting? This blog post covers how to create a RAP application in SAP BTP ABAP Environment  with a secondary HANA database connection.

 

Business scenario:


Many organizations already have business data stored in a separate SAP HANA Cloud database and want to build modern RAP (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model) applications on SAP BTP ABAP Environment without replicating that data into the ABAP system.

The blog demonstrates this using a Loyalty Management application.

Scenario

  • Customer loyalty information (Memberships) is stored in an external SAP HANA Cloud database.
  • Transaction data related to each membership is also stored in the same external database.
  • The business wants a Fiori application where users can:
    • Create loyalty memberships
    • Maintain loyalty transactions
    • Update and delete records
    • View accumulated loyalty points
    • Persist all changes directly in the external HANA database

 

Prerequisites:

  1. SAP BTP Sub-Account
  2. SAP HANA Cloud Instance
  3. SAP BTP ABAP Environment

Steps:

  1. Setup SAP HANA Cloud instance
  2. Create Schema and Tables
  3. Configure external connection to External HANA database from SAP BTP ABAP Environment using External Entities
  4. Create External Entities

Setup SAP HANA Cloud instance

Let’s start on with the creation of SAP HANA Cloud instance.

  1. Open SAP BTP cockpit and add the following Entitlements.



  2. Create HANA cloud subscription.


  3. Create HANA Cloud Instance


    Specify Instance name, Set DBADMIN password and choose Allow all IP address
    Spoiler
    Disclaimer on Database IP Access Configuration
    Allowing unrestricted access (e.g., permitting all IP addresses such as 0.0.0.0/0) to a database that contains production data introduces significant security risks. This approach should only be used in strictly controlled environments.


  4. Once the Instance status turns into Running state and click on Actions three dots and Open in SAP HANA Database Explorer. 
    Provide the DBADMIN credentials from the previous step to connect to the HANA database.


 

Create Schema and Tables

Open SQL Console and perform the following steps to create the schema and tables.
  • Create Schema

CREATE SCHEMA LOYALTY;​

  • Create Membership table: zlm_membship_ee  .

Stores membership details
Primary key: client + membershipuuid

create column table loyalty."zlm_membship_ee" (
"client" nvarchar (000003) default '000' not null, 
"membershipuuid" varbinary (000016) default x'00000000000000000000000000000000' not null , 
"membershipid" nvarchar (000010) default '0000000000' not null, 
"customer" nvarchar (000010) default '' not null, 
"createdby" nvarchar (000012) default '' not null, 
"createdat" decimal (000021, 000007) default 0 not null, 
"lastchangedby" nvarchar (000012) default '' not null, 
"locallastchangedat" decimal (000021, 000007) default 0 not null, 
"lastchangedat" decimal (000021, 000007) default 0 not null,
primary key ("client", "membershipuuid" )     
) column loadable
​
  • Create Transactions table: zlm_transctns_ee

Stores transactions linked to membership.
Contains transaction value and loyalty points.

create column table loyalty."zlm_transctns_ee" (
"client" nvarchar (000003) default '000' not null, 
"transactionuuid" varbinary (000016) default x'00000000000000000000000000000000' not null ,
"membershipuuid" varbinary (000016) default x'00000000000000000000000000000000' not null , 
"transactiondate" NVARCHAR (000008) DEFAULT '00000000' NOT NULL, 
"transactionvalue" DECIMAL (000010, 000002) DEFAULT 0 NOT NULL, 
"transactioncurrency" NVARCHAR (000005) DEFAULT '' NOT NULL, 
"loyaltypoints" BIGINT DEFAULT 0 NOT NULL, 
"createdby" nvarchar (000012) default '' not null, 
"createdat" decimal (000021, 000007) default 0 not null, 
"lastchangedby" nvarchar (000012) default '' not null, 
"locallastchangedat" decimal (000021, 000007) default 0 not null, 
"lastchangedat" decimal (000021, 000007) default 0 not null,
primary key ("client", "transactionuuid" )     
) column loadable

​


Configure connectivity to an external SAP HANA database from Steampunk for data access using External Entities

  1. Establish outbound SQL access using an external entity using ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse

    1. Define and activate a logical external schema whose configuration specifies the connection details for the outbound communication. Create a logical external schema in ABAP Development Tools (ADT) using the creation wizard.


    2.  Create an Outbound Service of the type Outbound SQL Access for your logical external schema.


    3. Create a Communication Scenario.


    4. Include the outbound service in the communication scenario.


  2. Configure External Connection
    In the Fiori Launchpad, Logon as Administrator and perform the following steps.
    1. Create a Communication Arrangement for scenario ZLM_COM_EXT_SCHEMA_EE.




    2.  Create a Communication System



      Toggle Remote SQL Access and choose Adapter Name as HANA (ODBC) 
      Adapter Configuration :Driver=libodbcHDB.so;ServerNode=*.hanacloud.ondemand.com(URL of the External Database):443;



    3. Create an Outbound User. Specify the HANA DB user which contains the necessary authorizations for accessing the External Schema.




Create CDS External Entities

Two types:
1. Writable External Entity → Used for INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE
2. Static External Entity → Used for SELECT (read only)

These entities map external tables to ABAP CDS.

In ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse: As an ABAP Developer, here’s what you need to do to establish outbound SQL access using a CDS external entity.

  • Define and activate an external entity that represents the external database object.As we are going to perform CUD operation on external database, we need to create Writable External Entity for table zlm_membship_ee which will be referred by external name zlm_membship_ee.
@EndUserText.label: 'Writable EE for HANA tab zlm_membship_ee'
define writable external entity zlm_membship_cud_ee external name "zlm_membship_ee"
{
  key client             : abap.char(3)                  external name "client";
  key membershipuuid     : sysuuid_x16                   external name "membershipuuid";
      membershipid       : zlm_membershipid_ee           external name "membershipid";
      customer           : zlm_customer_ee               external name "customer";
      @Semantics.user.createdBy: true
      createdby          : abp_creation_user             external name "createdby";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.createdAt: true
      createdat          : abp_creation_tstmpl           external name "createdat";
      @Semantics.user.lastChangedBy: true
      lastchangedby      : abp_lastchange_user           external name "lastchangedby";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.localInstanceLastChangedAt: true
      locallastchangedat : abp_locinst_lastchange_tstmpl external name "locallastchangedat";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.lastChangedAt: true
      lastchangedat      : abp_lastchange_tstmpl         external name "lastchangedat";
}
with federated data provided at runtime
​
​
  • We need to create Writable External Entity for table zlm_transctns_ee
@EndUserText.label: 'Writable EE for HANAtab zlm_transctns_ee'
define writable external entity zlm_transctns_cud_ee external name "zlm_transctns_ee"
{
  key client              : abap.char(3)                  external name "client";
  key transactionuuid     : sysuuid_x16                   external name "transactionuuid";
      membershipuuid      : sysuuid_x16                   external name "membershipuuid";
      transactiondate     : zlm_transactiondate_ee        external name "transactiondate";
      @Semantics.amount.currencyCode : 'transactioncurrency'
      transactionvalue    : zlm_transactionvalue_ee       external name "transactionvalue";
      transactioncurrency : zlm_transactioncurrency_ee    external name "transactioncurrency";
      loyaltypoints       : zlm_loyaltypoints_ee          external name "loyaltypoints";
      @Semantics.user.createdBy: true
      createdby           : abp_creation_user             external name "createdby";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.createdAt: true
      createdat           : abp_creation_tstmpl           external name "createdat";
      @Semantics.user.lastChangedBy: true
      lastchangedby       : abp_lastchange_user           external name "lastchangedby";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.localInstanceLastChangedAt: true
      locallastchangedat  : abp_locinst_lastchange_tstmpl external name "locallastchangedat";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.lastChangedAt: true
      lastchangedat       : abp_lastchange_tstmpl         external name "lastchangedat";
}
with federated data provided at runtime
​
​
  • To securely map a remote physical database object so it can be queried directly, we need to use STATIC EXTERNAL ENTITIES to fetch data from table zlm_membship_ee .
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Static EE for HANA tab zlm_membship_ee'
define external entity zlm_membship_ee external name "zlm_membship_ee"
{
  key client             : abap.char(3)                  external name "client";
  key membershipuuid     : sysuuid_x16                   external name "membershipuuid";
      membershipid       : zlm_membershipid_ee           external name "membershipid";
      customer           : zlm_customer_ee               external name "customer";
      @Semantics.user.createdBy: true
      createdby          : abp_creation_user             external name "createdby";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.createdAt: true
      createdat          : abp_creation_tstmpl           external name "createdat";
      @Semantics.user.lastChangedBy: true
      lastchangedby      : abp_lastchange_user           external name "lastchangedby";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.localInstanceLastChangedAt: true
      locallastchangedat : abp_locinst_lastchange_tstmpl external name "locallastchangedat";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.lastChangedAt: true
      lastchangedat      : abp_lastchange_tstmpl         external name "lastchangedat";
}
with federated data provided by zlm_ext_schema_ee
​
​
  • We need another STATIC EXTERNAL ENTITY for table zlm_transctns_ee
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Static EE for HANA tab zlm_transctns_ee'
define external entity zlm_transctns_ee external name "zlm_transctns_ee"
{
  key client              : abap.char(3)                  external name "client";
  key transactionuuid     : sysuuid_x16                   external name "transactionuuid";
      membershipuuid      : sysuuid_x16                   external name "membershipuuid";
      transactiondate     : zlm_transactiondate_ee        external name "transactiondate";
      @Semantics.amount.currencyCode : 'transactioncurrency'
      transactionvalue    : zlm_transactionvalue_ee       external name "transactionvalue";
      transactioncurrency : zlm_transactioncurrency_ee    external name "transactioncurrency";
      loyaltypoints       : zlm_loyaltypoints_ee          external name "loyaltypoints";
      @Semantics.user.createdBy: true
      createdby           : abp_creation_user             external name "createdby";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.createdAt: true
      createdat           : abp_creation_tstmpl           external name "createdat";
      @Semantics.user.lastChangedBy: true
      lastchangedby       : abp_lastchange_user           external name "lastchangedby";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.localInstanceLastChangedAt: true
      locallastchangedat  : abp_locinst_lastchange_tstmpl external name "locallastchangedat";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.lastChangedAt: true
      lastchangedat       : abp_lastchange_tstmpl         external name "lastchangedat";
}
with federated data provided by zlm_ext_schema_ee

​

Key Takeaways.

External entities enable real-time integration with external databases without replication.

In Part 2 of this series, we will build a RAP application using the data model created here.

entry.title: Start remote Process/Actions in BTP ABAP via Task Chains from SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
Modern cloud architectures allow for versatile and open interoperability. So does SAP Datasphere and SAP BTP ABAP, too. This example outlines some essentials how Datasphere can kick-off actions defined on the ABAP Cloud side, beyond system boundaries.

Starting a Task/Process in BTP ABAP remotely from SAP Datasphere using Task Chains opens up new scenarios for interoperability and integration. The goal is to orchestrate remote tasks initiated by SAP Datasphere — securely, programmatically, and fully automated.

We will cover the below points:

  • Creating a Task Chain in Datasphere including an API Call to ABAP Cloud
  • Connection in Datasphere to ABAP Cloud (for direct API call)
  • Setting up Communication System & Arrangement in ABAP Cloud
  • Executing the Task Chain in Datasphere: Trigger actions in ABAP Cloud

 

1 Architecture Overview

Architecture

The SAP Datasphere Task Chain API Task: Datasphere acts as the caller. ABAP Cloud exposes an endpoint (inbound Web Api) to trigger an action.

Option (2) and (3) complete the picture of possible solution approaches. Main differences as well as pros & cons are outlined further down in one of the last sections.

The Solution Architecture displays 3 different options:

  • (1) Use Direct API Integration 
    • Straightforward
    • Minimal cost and complexity
    • Perfect for nightly batch orchestration, event-driven data loads, or simple API-driven analytical data refresh
  • (2) Integration Suite
    • Integration involves complex message transformations, protocol mediation, multiple target systems, or when centralized API governance and monitoring across the enterprise are required.
    • Especially appropriate where iFlow reusability justifies the additional cost and setup effort or when integration suite is already in place and a common tool.
  • (3) CAP Application
    • Custom application logic is needed beyond what a direct API call/iFlow can provide
    • E.g. multi-source data aggregation, advanced parsing, enrichments
    • Additional persistence required
    • Well-suited for reusable microservice-style integrations on BTP

 

2 Prerequisites

You need

  1. SAP Datasphere tenant with developer + admin access
  2. Task Chain to design the flow of events
  3. SAP BTP ABAP Environment (ABAP Cloud) with developer access
  4. BTP ABAP: Authorization for OAuth clients, Communication Arrangements, ABAP development of Web APIs

 

 3 Creating Communication Artifacts in ABAP Cloud

Navigation: ABAP Environment Web Access → Communication Management → Communication Systems/Arrangements

Communication Arrangement

 

Communication Arrangement

Communication System

Communication System 1

Communication/Connection User:

Create a User for Inbound Communication and use the same in the subsequent step on Datasphere level for the creation of a generic HTTP connection.

 

4 ABAP Cloud Artifacts

For the sake of simplicity I won't go into the details of all of the development artifacts and configuration settings.

For sure, you will create:

  • Inbound Service
  • Service Binding
  • Communication Scenario BTP ABAP
  • Service Definition
  • Entity
  • Class implementation

An example of an action definition processData (ZI_DEMO_SERVICE):

managed implementation in class zbp_i_demo_service unique;

define behavior for ZI_DEMO_SERVICE alias DemoService
persistent table zdemoservice
lock master
{
  field ( readonly ) Id;
  field ( numbering : managed ) Id;

  create;
  update;
  delete;

  action processData result [1] zdemoservice;

  mapping for zdemoservice
  {
    Id = id;
    Name = name;
    Description = description;
    Status = status;
    CreatedBy = created_by;
    CreatedAt = created_at;
    LastChangedBy = last_changed_by;
    LastChangedAt = last_changed_at;
    LocalLastChanged = local_last_changed;
  }
}

An example of an ABAP Class with an action implementation:

CLASS lhc_DemoService DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_handler.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
 
    PRIVATE SECTION.
 
    METHODS processData FOR MODIFY
      IMPORTING keys FOR ACTION DemoService~processData result result.
 
ENDCLASS.
 
CLASS lhc_DemoService IMPLEMENTATION.
  
  METHOD processData.
    " Your business logic implementation her
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

Service Definition:

@EndUserText.label: 'Demo Service Definition'
define service ZUI_DEMO_SERVICE {
  expose ZC_DEMO_SERVICE as DemoService;
}

Inbound Service:

Communication Scenario:

 

5 Create a Connection

The first step is creating a generic HTTP connection to the endpoint exposed by ABAP Cloud. In order to do so, consider the following details. Take the connection host from the BTP ABAP instance key. The credentials must be taken from the connection user as defined in the communication arrangement.

 

An alternative to Username/Password is an OAuth based authentication mechanism. 

 

6 Creating a Task Chain in SAP Datasphere

The first step is defining a Task Chain that encapsulates the processing logic you want to execute .

In the given example:

  • The Task Chain starts with a API Task
  • Sends an email in case of success

Task Chain

In the API Task details, define the following:

  • Method POST
  • API Path - the path to the service endpoint of your ABAP Web API that you want to trigger
  • Mode Synchronous

API Task Details 1

 

Make sure to fetch the CSRF Token. The URL which I've used is simply the $metadata path of the service endpoint:

https://<ABAP_HOST>.abap.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/sap/opu/odata/sap/ZUI_DEMO_SERVICE_O4/$metadata

 

7 Execution Results

The Task Chain in Datasphere successfully executed the ABAP Web API call:

Task Chain API successfully executed

For monitoring and troubleshooting purpose, individual API calls can be traced from BTP ABAP side. The HTTP trace can be turned on for granular levels.

 

8 Solution Options - Comparisons

The following comparison outlines the key aspects per solution/architecture option as depicted in the initial architecture diagram. I have prototyped all of the below and all options are working flawlessly. Especially w.r.t. to the CAP based solution, there is a wide open room to play with.

 

Option

Pros (+)

Cons (-)

(1) Direct API

  • Simplest setup & fewest components & lowest cost
  • No middleware latency
  • Lightweight and developer-friendly
  • Limited transformation capability
  • Less suitable for multi-step or conditional routing logic

(2) Integration Suite

  • Supports complex transformations, multiple protocols and error handling in iFlows
  • Centralized API governance via API Management
  • Recommended for complex mediated flows
  • Two-step connectivity required (Integration Suite + Datasphere)
  • Additional licensing cost
  • Higher setup and maintenance effort

(3) CAP CF App

  • Maximum flexibility for custom logic (parsing, filtering, enrichment, retry)
  • CAP integrates natively with HANA Cloud/Datasphere HDI
  • Supports secure credential management
  • Reusable microservice pattern
  • Requires application development and BTP CF deployment skills
  • Higher operational overhead
  • More BTP resource consumption
  • CAP app lifecycle must be managed independently

 

9 Conclusion/Outlook & Further aspects:

Overview of the major building blocks:

Capability

Result

No hardcoded secrets

via Communication Arrangements/System

Remote orchestration

Creating an end-to-end orchestrated chain of tasks, bidirectional if required

Cloud-native ABAP

Using appropriate ABAP Class APIs

Datasphere automation

Using Task Chain API

 

Use Cases that the implementation enables:

  • Nightly batch orchestration
  • Event-driven data loads
  • Cross-system workflows (this was our ultimate focus)
  • API-driven (transactional/analytical) data refresh

Limitations:

  • The Task Chain/API Task can't be parameterized dynamically with runtime parameters to e.g. initiate calls that have a variable payload in header or body
  • Moreover, the write-back/storing of HTTP responses is limited too

References:

  • SAP Datasphere Task Chain API Documentation:  https://api.sap.com/api/DatasphereTasks/overview
  • SAP Datasphere Task Chain Help Page: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_DATASPHERE/c8a54ee704e94e15926551293243fd1d/274f2736465c4c48a091c67588...

This blog entry complements my other blog on Triggering SAP Datasphere/BDC Task Chains from SAP BTP ABAP Cloud using the Task Chain REST API.

In case of questions feel free to raise them and please leave a comment if you found something incorrect - thanks for any help on that. I'm hoping this blog can support somebody out there and may facilitate some problem solving.

entry.title: Clean Core Levels A–D: how to classify your custom ABAP — and what to do with each grade
entry.content:
Disclosure: I'm the author of this post and the creator of Clean-Core.io, a free, non-commercial community tool I mention once near the end. This post is educational; the tool is optional.

Overview

With the 2025 update to the ABAP extensibility guide, SAP evolved the familiar 3-tier extensibility model into the clean core level concept — a four-grade classification of custom extensions: A, B, C, D. Instead of a blunt "clean vs. not clean", it tells you how closely each extension aligns with Clean Core, and it maps directly to ABAP Test Cockpit (ATC) behaviour:

 

Level Usage of SAP objects in custom development ATC behaviour

AReleased SAP APIs (local & remote) and extension pointsNo message
BClassic SAP APIs and extension points (SAP-recommended)Priority 3 · info
CInternal SAP APIs (conditionally clean via the changelog-for-SAP-objects check)Priority 2 · warning
DNot-recommended objects & extension technologies (modifications, implicit enhancements)Priority 1 · error

Level A is the cleanest — on-stack ABAP Cloud or side-by-side on SAP BTP. Levels B–D describe classic ABAP, from SAP-recommended (B) down to not-recommended (D).

Why this matters now

Every custom object you carry into S/4HANA has to be defensible. The move from a binary "clean/not clean" to A–D is a gift — it lets you transform at your own pace, prioritising the worst first instead of trying to reach "100% clean" overnight.

The real problem

Knowing the model is easy. Grading thousands of custom objects against it is not. ATC flags readiness object-by-object, and the Cloudification Repository tells you which standard objects are released — but turning that into a per-object A/B/C/D verdict plus a remediation path, across a grown Z-landscape, is still mostly manual and inconsistent between teams.

A practical classification flow

  1. Identify the custom object and what it touches (tables, APIs, function modules, enhancements).
  2. Look up each dependency: released API/extension point → A; classic-but-official API → B; internal SAP object → C; modification / not-recommended access → D.
  3. Decide remediation per grade: A keep · B keep, plan to lift later · C verify via the changelog-for-SAP-objects approach and wrap behind a released interface · D replace before the upgrade.

A worked example

A classic Z_CUSTOMER_REPORT:

SELECT kunnr, name1 FROM kna1 INTO TABLE @lt_cust.   " direct read of an internal standard table

Classify: direct access to KNA1 (an internal SAP object, not a released API) → Level C (ATC: warning). A direct write would be Level D (ATC: error).

Remediate: map to the released CDS view I_Customer → the same logic becomes Level A (ATC: no message):

SELECT Customer, CustomerName FROM I_Customer INTO TABLE @lt_cust.

One dependency, one grade, one clear next step. Repeat across the landscape and you get a prioritised backlog: fix D first (upgrade blockers), lift C, schedule B, keep A.

How I approached tooling this (optional)

To avoid doing this by hand, I built Clean-Core.io — a free, non-commercial community tool. It runs a deterministic evidence engine first (parsing the code, mapping dependencies against the catalog), then AI on top, and derives an A–D grade per detected object — aligned to the clean core level concept and its ATC priorities. It is complementary to ATC/ADT, not a replacement, and every output is a draft for a qualified architect to review. Free: clean-core.io (link in the comments).

Conclusion

The move from 3-tier to A–D isn't a relabel — it's a better mental model for prioritising custom-code remediation. Give each dependency an explicit grade, start with your Level D objects, and turn "we have a lot of Z-code" into a defensible, upgrade-ready plan.

How are you classifying your custom code against the new A–D levels today — by hand, ATC, or something else? I'd genuinely like to compare approaches in the comments.

entry.title: RAP Augmentation in Managed Scenario – Enriching Transactional Requests Before BO Processing
entry.content:
Introduction

The RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP) provides several extension points for implementing business logic, such as Determinations, Validations, Actions, and Prechecks. RAP Augmentation adds another extension point that allows developers to enrich incoming create or update requests before the framework starts processing the business object.

This early execution makes augmentation useful for scenarios where default values, audit information, or business-specific data must be available before any further RAP processing takes place. In this blog, we will understand RAP Augmentation, compare it with Determinations, and implement it using an Employee Management example.

Business Requirement

I had a requirement  an Employee Management application where users create and maintain employee records. During record creation, the application should automatically populate business-specific information instead of relying on users to enter every value manually.

The application should:

  • Set the employee status to PENDING_APPROVAL.
  • Determine whether approval is required based on the selected department.
  • Capture the current user as the creator.
  • Maintain audit information during updates.

Instead of implementing all this logic inside the business object, we will use RAP Augmentation to enrich the incoming request before standard processing begins.

Problem Statement

Determinations are commonly used in RAP to derive or update field values. However, they execute after the request has already entered the transactional buffer.

In some business scenarios, values must be available even before the business object starts processing. For example, default values or audit fields may be required before validations or other business logic execute. In such cases, Augmentation provides a cleaner and more suitable solution by enriching the request at the beginning of the transaction.

What is RAP Augmentation?

RAP Augmentation is an extension mechanism that runs during the Interactive Phase of a RAP transaction. It allows developers to enrich incoming create or update requests before the framework processes the business object.

Rather than modifying persisted data, augmentation focuses on preparing the request by supplying additional values or applying business-specific logic. Once the request is enriched, RAP continues with its normal processing flow.

Augmentation Flow

The request passes through augmentation before reaching the business object implementation. During this stage, additional values can be added or modified, ensuring the business object receives a complete request for further processing.

The overall execution flow is:

  • User sends a create or update request.
  • RAP invokes the registered augmentation.
  • The request is enriched with additional data.
  • Standard RAP processing continues.
  • Data is persisted to the database.

Augmentation vs Determination

Defined at the Projection Behavior Definition using use create/update (augment).Defined at the Interface Behavior Definition using  determination.
Implemented in a Projection Behavior Implementation Class Implemented in the Behavior Pool  class.
Runs during the Interactive Phase, before the BO implementation processes the request.Runs during ON MODIFY or ON SAVE, after data enters the transactional buffer.
Used to enrich or modify the incoming request before BO processing.Used to derive or recalculate values based on data in the transactional buffer.

When Execution timing matter?

The main advantage of Augmentation is when it executes. Since it runs before business object processing, any values added during augmentation are available to the rest of the RAP processing flow.

  • If the application should automatically set an employee's status or determine whether approval is required, these values are already present when the framework performs subsequent processing. If the same logic were implemented in a Determination, the values would only be available later in the transaction.

When to Use Augmentation?

Augmentation is a good choice when the incoming request needs to be enriched before RAP starts processing the business object.

Typical use cases include:

  • Setting default values.
  • Populating audit fields.
  • Deriving values from user input.
  • Performing lightweight request enrichment.
  • Preparing data required by later processing steps.

It is not intended to replace Determinations, which remain the preferred option for business logic that should execute after the request has entered the transactional buffer.

 

BDEF of Interface

managed implementation in class zbp_aky_i_emp unique;
strict ( 2 );

define behavior for zaky_i_emp //alias <alias_name>
persistent table zaky_t_emp

lock master

authorization master ( instance )
//etag master <field_name>
{
  create( authorization : global ) ;
  update;
  delete;
  action Approve result [1] $self;
  mapping for zaky_t_emp
{
  Employeeid        = employeeid;
  Firstname         = firstname;
  Lastname          = lastname;
  Department        = department;
  Status            = status;
  Approvalrequired  = approvalrequired;
  Createdby         = createdby;
  Createdat         = createdat;
  Lastchangedby     = lastchangedby;
  Lastchangedat     = lastchangedat;
  }
}

BDEF of Projection

projection implementation in class zbp_aky_c_emp unique;
strict ( 2 );

define behavior for zaky_c_emp //alias <alias_name>
{
  use create( augment );
  use update( augment );
    use delete;
  field ( readonly ) Status;
  field ( readonly ) Approvalrequired;
  field ( readonly ) Createdby;
  field ( readonly ) Createdat;
  field ( readonly ) Lastchangedby;
  field ( readonly ) Lastchangedat;
  use action Approve;
}

BP of Projection

CLASS lhc_zaky_c_emp DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_handler.
  PRIVATE SECTION.

    METHODS augment_create FOR MODIFY
      IMPORTING entities FOR CREATE zaky_c_emp.

    METHODS augment_update FOR MODIFY
      IMPORTING entities FOR UPDATE zaky_c_emp.

ENDCLASS.

CLASS lhc_zaky_c_emp IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD augment_create.
  LOOP AT entities INTO DATA(entity).

    IF entity-Department IS INITIAL.
      APPEND VALUE #(
        %cid = entity-%cid
        %msg = new_message_with_text(
                 severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error
                 text     = 'Department is mandatory' ) )
      TO reported-zaky_c_emp.        

      APPEND VALUE #( %cid = entity-%cid )
      TO failed-zaky_c_emp.          
      CONTINUE.
    ENDIF.

    MODIFY AUGMENTING ENTITIES OF zaky_i_emp  
      ENTITY zaky_i_emp
      CREATE FIELDS ( Status Approvalrequired Createdby Createdat )
      WITH VALUE #( (
        %cid             = entity-%cid
        Status           = 'PENDING_APPROVAL'
        Approvalrequired = COND #(
                             WHEN entity-Department = 'HR'
                               OR entity-Department = 'FIN'
                             THEN abap_true
                             ELSE abap_false )
        Createdby        = sy-uname
        Createdat        = cl_abap_context_info=>get_system_date(  )
      ) ).

  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

METHOD augment_update.
  LOOP AT entities INTO DATA(entity).

    MODIFY AUGMENTING ENTITIES OF zaky_i_emp   
      ENTITY zaky_i_emp
      UPDATE FIELDS ( Lastchangedby Lastchangedat )
      WITH VALUE #( (
        Employeeid    = entity-Employeeid
        Lastchangedby = sy-uname
        Lastchangedat = cl_abap_context_info=>get_system_date(  )
      ) ).

  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

BP of interface

METHOD Approve.

  " Read current status of the employee
  READ ENTITIES OF zaky_i_emp IN LOCAL MODE
    ENTITY zaky_i_emp
    FIELDS ( Status Employeeid )
    WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys )
    RESULT DATA(employees)
    FAILED DATA(read_failed).

  " Loop and update status
  LOOP AT employees INTO DATA(employee).

    " Only approve if current status is PENDING_APPROVAL
    IF employee-Status <> 'PENDING_APPROVAL'.
      APPEND VALUE #(
        %tky = employee-%tky
        %msg = new_message_with_text(
                 severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error
                 text     = 'Only records with PENDING_APPROVAL status can be approved' ) )
      TO reported-zaky_i_emp.
      APPEND VALUE #( %tky = employee-%tky )
      TO failed-zaky_i_emp.
      CONTINUE.
    ENDIF.

    " Update status to APPROVED
    MODIFY ENTITIES OF zaky_i_emp IN LOCAL MODE
      ENTITY zaky_i_emp
      UPDATE FIELDS ( Status )
      WITH VALUE #( (
        %tky   = employee-%tky
        Status = 'APPROVED'
      ) )
      FAILED   DATA(update_failed)
      REPORTED DATA(update_reported).

    " Pass back result
    APPEND VALUE #(
      %tky   = employee-%tky
      %param = employee
    ) TO result.

  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

Output

Click on the create 

After adding the value click on the create and put the debugger on the create-augment method to check it is triggering or not because we are doing managed application where creation is handled by framework

Execute

Currently it is in pending state click on the Approve employee.

After clicking on action

If you not adding the department

 

Conclusion

RAP Augmentation provides a simple and effective way to enrich transactional requests before the RAP framework begins processing the business object. It is particularly useful for populating default values, maintaining audit information, and preparing request data that should be available throughout the transaction.

In this example, we used augmentation to automatically populate employee details while keeping the business object implementation clean and maintainable. Understanding when to use Augmentation instead of Determinations helps developers choose the right extension point and build more maintainable RAP applications

entry.title: Secure Outbound Communication in ABAP Cloud
entry.content:
Introduction

Modern ABAP development, especially in cloud environments, is increasingly centered around one core idea: while application logic still triggers and executes the outbound calls, the underlying communication configuration and connection settings must be strictly separated from the application logic to ensure secure and controlled integration. Whether an application calls an external HTTP service or an RFC-enabled system, the goal is no longer just to make the call succeed, but to ensure that every connection is governed, traceable, and aligned with clean-core principles.

SAP addresses this through a structured communication model in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, SAP BTP ABAP environment and SAP S/4HHANA Private Cloud Edition.

The model is based on two pillars: The development object Communication Target (CoTa) and the configuration stored in so called Application Destinations.

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition (and in on-premise), integrations require both the communication target and the application destinations to be maintained explicitly. In contrast, for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and SAP BTP ABAP Environment, the application destination is generated automatically when the administrator saves the communication arrangement, further simplifying configuration and lifecycle management. While the system handles the creation of the destination automatically, meaning customers do not need to write custom code to save the destination itself, users retain full control over where the automatically generated destination name is stored and maintained within the system (see Executing Custom Code During a Communication Arrangement Save | SAP Help Portal )

While ABAP Cloud preserves the principle of separating configuration from application logic and still requires code adaptations when communication protocols change (for example, from RFC to HTTP), it significantly modernizes connectivity management.

Communication targets replace this approach with a formal development object that represents the intended communication purpose. Rather than referencing a technical destination directly, application code refers to the communication target, and the runtime framework resolves the underlying destination. This automation applies when there is only a single application destination, in other words, the application logic remains completely unaware of the application destination name.

If multiple application destinations exist, the specific name of the application destination must still be explicitly provided in the code, just as with a traditional SM59 destination.

Communication targets also act as a logical control layer that governs access to destinations. Unlike the traditional model, where any application could potentially reuse a destination if its name was known, communication targets explicitly define which applications are allowed to use a particular destination. This prevents uncontrolled reuse and establishes a clearer and more governed communication model.

By enforcing this explicit authorization, the model mitigates critical security risks, such as:

  • Data Spoofing & Interception: Unauthorized applications can no longer masquerade as legitimate services to intercept sensitive data streams.
  • Resource Depletion (DoS): Rogue or compromised applications are prevented from flooding a destination with traffic, ensuring high availability for authorized users.
  • Privilege Escalation: It eliminates the risk of an application accidentally (or maliciously) gaining access to high-privilege backend systems simply by guessing a destination name.

More in Communication Target | SAP Help Portal and Using Communication Targets | SAP Help Portal

Although both communication targets and SM59 destinations separate configuration from business logic, communication targets extend this concept through stronger lifecycle integration, improved traceability, controlled destination access, and greater flexibility in managing tenant-specific configuration.

Besides, communication targets deliver key architectural upgrades by utilizing an object-oriented class basis. Because all communication is routed through communication targets, the user can perform reliable where-used analysis and runtime validation. In addition, communication targets allow developers to define general communication settings and client dependency as part of the development object, reducing configuration errors and simplifying administration. These two advantages represent the core improvements of the new concept.

HTTP Communication via Communication Targets

For HTTP communication, this model is reinforced by communication target. Each outbound call is validated at runtime to ensure that the application is authorized to use the destination behind the communication target. This major improvement over the classic model significantly strengthens security by design.

From a development perspective, the beauty of this model is its simplicity in code. Once a communication arrangement is defined and the application destination is generated, the ABAP implementation becomes clean and focused purely on business logic. All technical details such as URLs, authentication, and certificates, are handled outside the application.
In case there will be only one application destination (most likely case), a typical HTTP call in ABAP Cloud looks like below.

DATA: lo_cota TYPE REF TO ZCT_EXAMPLE.

lo_cota = NEW ZCT_EXAMPLE(application_destination = 'Z_APPLDEST_EXAMPLE' ).

DATA(lo_client) = lo_cota->create_web_http_client( ).

DATA(lo_response) = lo_client->execute( if_web_http_client=>get ).

However, if multiple application destinations are available for the Communication target, you must explicitly pass the name of the relevant application destination as a parameter to ensure the outbound call maps to the correct target system.

The code is intentionally minimal. For example, in Cloud, the application. This ensures that the application can only communicate with endpoints that are explicitly approved.

RFC Communication via Communication Targets

The same principle applies to RFC communication: for outbound RFC calls, you should use communication targets together with the application destination. The same principle applies to RFC communication: for outbound RFC calls, it is recommended to use communication targets together with the application destination.
The architectural lifecycle of application destinations is conceptually identical for both RFC and HTTP protocols; in both cases, the communication target serves as the abstract reference in your ABAP code, while administrators manage the destination details. The distinction lies purely in the developer implementation at runtime, where protocol-specific configuration and distinct APIs, such as classic function modules for RFC versus HTTP client classes for REST, must be used to execute the call.

In the newer governance model, RFC connections can be governed through communication arrangements and communication targets, providing the same structured, secure, and centrally managed approach that is already used for HTTP communication. In code, the call may still look familiar:

DATA: lo_cota TYPE REF TO ZCT_EXAMPLE.
    lo_cota = NEW ZCT_EXAMPLE( ).
    DATA(lo_remote_session) = lo_cota->create_session( ).
    CALL FUNCTION 'RFC_PING' IN REMOTE SESSION lo_remote_session.

The important difference is not only in the governance of the destination, but also in the syntax itself, which is an important aspect of the new concept. A session object is created from the corresponding CoTa class. With this class-based approach, developers can perform a where-used analysis on the class. This reflects a broader shift in ABAP development. Developers are no longer responsible for managing connectivity details directly. Instead, they consume centrally defined communication configurations. This separation improves maintainability and allows changes to endpoints or credentials without modifying application code.

Cloud VS On-Premise

In ABAP Cloud, communication target is highly recommended and preferred. Within the Communication Management framework of ABAP Cloud, utilizing a formal communication target is highly recommended and considered best practice. However, while the structured framework is preferred for most scenarios, it is still technically possible to establish outbound connections independently when needed. Developers can bypass the standard communication target lifecycle by dynamically instantiating destinations at runtime using specific provider methods, such as creating a destination directly via a URL or a BTP Cloud Destination.


In on-premise systems, the landscape is even more flexible, as they can also leverage modern cloud connectivity concepts. Starting with the SAP S/4HANA 2025 release, on-premises systems support modern connectivity features, including the use of communication targets alongside application destinations (APPLDEST) for outbound HTTP and RFC communication. Meanwhile, the traditional approach using transaction SM59 remains available, particularly for earlier on-premises releases or classic scenarios.
Existing SM59 configurations can also be migrated to application destination for an existing  HTTP or RFC applications. However, the use of communication targets is not enforced system-wide in on-premises environments, unlike in ABAP Cloud.
By early adoption of communication targets, even in on-premises landscapes, developers can align their applications with a model that is clearly designed for the future, secure, transparent, and ready for cloud-native integration.

Further studies

If you want to explore this concept in detail, SAP provides both official documentation and deep-dive blogs:

SAP Help Portal:

  • https://help.sap.com/docs/abap-cloud/abap-integration-connectivity/enable-http-communication-in-your-abap-code
  • Outbound Communication | SAP Help Portal
  • https://help.sap.com/docs/abap-cloud/abap-integration-connectivity/consuming-rfc-service

SAP Community blogs:

  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/receiver-validation-http-enhance-your-security-by-controlling-destination/ba-p/13972704
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/using-communication-targets-for-outbound-rfc-communication-in-abap/ba-p/14261486

entry.title: Early idea: VS Code extension merging ADT + abaplint, is this useful to anyone?
entry.content:
Hey SAP Community,

I got tired of the usual ABAP loop. Write something, hit Ctrl+F3, wait a
few seconds, see the error, fix it, repeat. So I started building
something to get rid of the waiting part.

The idea is simple: run abaplint locally so you get errors instantly while
typing, and when you're ready, send the code to SAP with one command
instead of switching to GUI.

Right now it has inline diagnostics while typing, basic syntax
highlighting, a code outline panel showing classes and methods, keyword
autocomplete, and an activate command that talks to ADT.

I only tested it against a local mock server, not a real SAP system. Auth
for BTP is not properly handled yet. There's no autocomplete from the
actual system. It probably breaks on edge cases I haven't thought of.

I'm posting because I genuinely don't know if this is useful or if someone
already did this better. If the idea resonates, the code is open and the
architecture is meant to be extended. Take it, fork it, break it, improve
it.

GitHub: https://github.wdf.sap.corp/I749493/abap-adv-linter

Honest feedback appreciated, including "this already exists, look here
instead."

Thanks,

entry.title: ABAP development tools for Visual Studio Code: Version 1.1 released
entry.content:
What happened since the last release?

Link to the extension.

First of all, thanks for the positive feedback regarding the 1.0.1 patch which added the much requested RFC + password support! Please keep providing feedback as it helps us to plan the next releases. A lot of users are asking us how they can provide feedback. We have recently added a new customer influence channel just for the VS Code extension. We recommend using that one.

Last month, ABAPConf 2026 took place in Mannheim. We want to highlight two talks:

  1. Tobias Hofmann provided public feedback regarding the extension and compiled a nice to-do list. I can report that a lot of these to-do items are already part of our internal to-do list. The talk can be found here.
  2. Anne Keller and I gave a talk on the overall ABAP IDE strategy. Of course, it touches the VS Code extension but also aims to provide a broader picture on how we want to support ABAP IDEs via a single technology stack in the future and shares some insights on how the ADT MCP server will evolve in the future. The talk can be found here.

 

What gets shipped with this release?

Usability improvements for system connection handling

After creating a new destination there was no visual feedback that the destination was created successfully. We added a notification pop-up with a button which directly adds it as a folder to your workspace.

We added a "Log on to Destination..." command. While it was possible to log on to a destination via a right-click menu in the explorer view there was no command available for it.

Now, you can easily log on to a destination using the keyboard via the new command.

A significant improvement of object type support

A lot of requested object types get enabled by this release. Here is the list of added object types:

Classic ABAP source code
  • Create and Manage Function Groups and Modules
  • Create and Manage ABAP Programs and Includes
  • Create and Manage Lock Objects
Data Dictionary
  • Create and Manage Database Tables
  • Create and Manage Structures
  • Create and Manage Type Groups
RESTful Application Programming Model
  • Creating Behavior Definition Extensions
  • Enabling more OData Binding Types in Service Binding
  • Create and Manage CDS Entity Buffers

New MCP tools

Unified diff tool for transports

The unified diff tool generates a git like diff for a given transport request. The tool enables token efficient analysis of the changes contained in a transport request. This enables AI agents to fulfill requests such as:

  • "Explain transport XYZ"
  • "Generate a description for transport XYZ documenting what got changed and why"
  • "How much impact does transport XYZ has on the system? How dangerous is this transport?"
Tools for ABAP Test Cockpit (ATC)

The ATC MCP tools give AI agents access to running the ATC checks for a given change. This enables AI agents to fulfill requests such as:

  • "Implement [XYZ]. Afterwards, run the ATC checks and fix the reported findings."
  • "Run ATC checks for object XYZ"

Additional UX improvements

We added a command which allows you to quickly open the SAP Help documentation.

From each occurrence of a CDS annotation in a CDS data definition or CDS metadata extension, you can now navigate to its CDS annotation definition. To navigate, press F3 on the relevant CDS annotation. This helps you to investigate and to understand the use of a CDS annotation.

For more information see the new "What's New" page.

 

Sidenote: first version of ABAP cleaner VS Code extension available

Lots of community members asked for ABAP cleaner support in VS Code. We are happy to announce that the first version of the ABAP cleaner extension is available, now!

 

What's next?

From now on, we will stop listing upcoming features directly in the release blogs. Instead we have cleaned up and refreshed our ABAP Cloud Roadmap page. The planned features are listed there.

Provide feedback and influence our product backlog!

We welcome your feedback! Feel free to use the comment section or create a customer influence request to let us know which features you are missing the most. Your input will help us prioritize the feature backlog for the next releases.
